does: This paper examines the problem of aligning AI systems with human values, arguing that the increasing power and autonomy of AI systems creates novel challenges distinct from prior technology alignment efforts. It synthesizes insights from two previously disconnected research communities: the fairness, accountability, transparency, and ethics (FATE) community focused on algorithmic bias and societal impacts, and technical AI safety researchers concerned with value specification and control. The paper's central contribution is identifying and articulating the underexplored problem of 'social value alignment'—how to align AI systems not with singular predetermined values but with the plurality and diversity of values held across global populations and groups.
connects: This work bridges two historically separate scholarly traditions. It builds on decades of technology studies examining how systems 'lock in' particular value systems and constrain future possibilities, alongside participatory design literature showing how stakeholder involvement shapes technology. It connects to recent work in algorithmic fairness and FATE addressing concrete harms from biased systems, complementing narrow technical definitions of fairness with broader ethical concerns. Simultaneously, it engages with emerging technical AI safety literature on value learning and specification. The paper responds to the recognition that previous technology alignment frameworks are insufficient for autonomous AI systems, synthesizing rather than replacing either tradition.
enables: This framing enables several future research directions. It suggests that purely technical approaches to value alignment, whether in fairness or safety, must incorporate insights from participatory governance and multi-stakeholder deliberation. It opens investigation into how AI systems can be designed to remain responsive to evolving and contested value pluralism rather than embedding fixed preferences. The work points toward hybrid methodologies combining technical safety research with inclusive design processes and democratic inputs. It establishes a research agenda for understanding how global coordination on AI values might occur despite deep disagreements, and how to design systems that avoid entrenchment of particular value configurations while remaining steerable and accountable.
